game_name: Quest for Glory: So You Want to Be a Hero

design_philosophy.core_loop: Accept quests from NPCs, build character stats through usage (magic increases Intelligence, combat increases Strength/Agility), solve problems with acquired skills, earn experience and gold to purchase equipment, progress toward defeating Baba Yaga and breaking her curse on Spielburg Valley
design_philosophy.primary_tension: The land is cursed and ravaged by monsters and brigands; the player must grow strong enough and skilled enough to confront increasingly difficult challenges while managing time (day/night cycles) and basic survival needs (eating, sleeping)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Become a Hero by growing from a recent graduate of the Famous Adventurer's Correspondence School into a legendary figure capable of solving complex problems and breaking curses through exploration, combat, and magical study
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Hybrid adventure-RPG that ties stat growth directly to actual gameplay actions rather than level-ups; offers three distinct character classes (Fighter, Magic User, Thief) with fundamentally different puzzle-solving approaches and quest lines, yet allows cross-class skill acquisition; implements a living world with time-passing mechanics, NPC schedules, day/night cycles, and consequences (character must eat and sleep); open-ended quest structure where player can tackle objectives in any order; emphasizes story as equally important as combat and exploration

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Significant agency through character class selection at start; Fighter, Magic User, and Thief classes approach problems differently, allowing players to self-select difficulty based on their preferred playstyle; optional secondary quests provide additional challenge and reward without mandatory completion; open quest ordering allows players to tackle challenges in preferred sequence

progression.upgrade_system: Stats-driven progression system where Strength, Intelligence, Health (Vitality), and Agility increase through organic use: magic usage increases Magic ability and Intelligence; combat training and fighting increases Strength, Vitality, and Agility; even menial tasks like cleaning stables builds Strength. Equipment purchases available through earned gold. Character skills expand through practice and quest completion
progression.unlock_mechanism: Quest completion grants experience and gold; certain quests and areas unlock based on character class and stat thresholds; solving quests for NPCs opens new narrative pathways and unlocks optimal ending conditions
replayability_hooks: Three distinct character classes with different equipment, puzzle solutions, and quest lines
Cross-class skill acquisition allows varied builds across playthroughs
Open quest structure enables different completion orders
Multiple ending states ranging from partial completion to optimal ending that unlocks sequel content
Day/night cycle mechanics and NPC schedules create dynamic world state

world_building_techniques: Dialogue with NPCs (player inputs commands like 'ask about brigands')
Environmental storytelling through exploration of cursed valley with visible monster/brigand damage
NPC interaction and quest-giving reveals plot through conversation
Folklore-sourced setting and creatures (Baba Yaga, dryads, hermits, fantasy creatures called Meeps)
Time-passing mechanics reveal story changes: day/night transitions, character transformation outcomes, curse manifestations
Series continuity through recurring characters (Erasmus the wizard, Fenrus familiar, references to benevolent faery Erana) and prophecy fulfillment leading into sequel

boss_design.philosophy: Central narrative antagonist (Baba Yaga, the evil ogress) represents the curse afflicting the land; defeating her is the ultimate goal tied to breaking the curse and fulfilling prophecy. Boss encounters likely tied to stat thresholds and class-specific approaches given the game's core design
notable_bosses: Baba Yaga (primary antagonist; evil ogress who cursed the land and the Baron; must be defeated to achieve optimal ending)
Brigand leader (revealed to be the Baron's cursed daughter Elsa von Spielburg; player must free her from curse)

faction_indicators: Choice of character class (Fighter, Magic User, Thief) signals player identity and preferred problem-solving approach
Stat distribution reflects player playstyle and values (investing in Strength vs. Intelligence vs. Agility)
Quest completion choices and order reveal player moral alignment and priorities
danger_signals: Brigands and monsters ravaging the valley represent immediate physical danger
Baba Yaga's curse is an existential threat to the land (Baron cursed, daughter transformed into brigand leader, son transformed into bear)
Time pressure implied through day/night cycles and survival needs (eating, sleeping)
environmental_storytelling: Spielburg Valley shows visible signs of curse and monster ravagement throughout exploration
NPC locations and availability shift with day/night cycles, reflecting a living world
Physical transformation of cursed individuals (Baron's son as bear, daughter as brigand leader) serves as visual narrative markers
Magical carpet departure at story conclusion signals transition to sequel setting

sources_used: Wikipedia: Quest for Glory: So You Want to Be a Hero
